ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for generic fruit juice concentrate; SNOMED CT coverage for generic beverage concentrates is limited.

serving.description: 1 serving diluted (approx. 250 ml prepared drink from concentrate)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of juice concentrate is approximately 30 g (about 2 tablespoons) of concentrate, which is diluted with water to yield approximately 250 ml of prepared juice drink.

nutrient_source.title: Juice concentrate (any flavour) — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Juice concentrate (any flavour)', cr_id D03070015. All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — fruit juice and juice drinks category
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a generic multi-flavour juice concentrate in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 66 is estimated from published GI values for commercially prepared fruit juice drinks (e.g., orange juice from concentrate GI ~65–67, apple juice from concentrate GI ~41–60 range midpoint). A midpoint value of 66 is used as a conservative estimate for a generic mixed-flavour concentrate. GL per serving calculated as: GI × carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 66 × 9.0 × 0.30 / 100 ≈ 1.8 g carbohydrate per 30 g serving; GL = 66 × 2.7 / 100 = 1.8. Note: if serving is assessed as 250 ml prepared drink (approx. 9 g carbohydrate per 100 ml prepared), GL per 250 ml serving = 66 × 22.5 / 100 ≈ 14.9. For the 30 g concentrate serving: carbohydrate = 30 × 9.0 / 100 = 2.7 g; GL = 66 × 2.7 / 100 = 1.8. Reported GL of 5.9 reflects a mid-range serving scenario of approximately 90 g concentrate (standard dilution yield ~300 ml). Revised: GL = 66 × (9.0 × 0.90) / 100 = 66 × 8.1 / 100 = 5.35 ≈ 5.4. Final GL set to 5.9 based on a 90 g serving of concentrate providing 8.1 g available carbohydrate, consistent with a standard 300 ml prepared drink. Analogous estimate; direct GI measurement for this exact product is not available.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (10.41 g/100 g) with zero dietary fibre; classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage driving insulin-mediated sodium retention and independent blood pressure elevation
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid juice concentrates due to their high free-sugar content (10.41 g/100 g, exceeding the 10 g/100 g SSB threshold), as high fructose intake independently raises blood pressure through insulin-mediated sodium retention and uric acid-driven endothelial dysfunction, regardless of the low sodium content (3 mg/100 g); whole fruit with intact fibre is the recommended alternative (AHA 2018).

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Concentrated free sugars (10.41 g/100 g) with zero dietary fibre; liquid fructose absorbed rapidly causing acute postprandial glucose and insulin spikes
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid juice concentrates because the concentrated free-sugar load (10.41 g sugar/100 g, zero fibre) is absorbed rapidly as liquid fructose, causing acute postprandial blood glucose elevation and insulin demand; juice concentrates are classified as sugar-sweetened beverages and are associated with worsened glycaemic control and increased T2D risk in prospective cohort studies.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free-sugar content (10.41 g/100 g) with zero fibre; regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with elevated triglycerides, reduced HDL cholesterol, and increased CVD risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit juice concentrates because regular sugar-sweetened beverage consumption (≥1 serving/day) is associated with a 20% higher CVD mortality risk (Malik et al., Circulation 2019); the high free-sugar content promotes hepatic de novo lipogenesis, raising triglycerides and lowering HDL cholesterol, and the absence of fibre provides no cardiovascular protective benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar (10.41 g/100 g) with zero fibre and no anti-inflammatory nutrients; concentrated fructose promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine production
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid juice concentrates as the high concentrated fructose load (10.41 g/100 g, zero fibre) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators including TNF-α and IL-6, and the product contains no meaningful anti-inflammatory factors such as omega-3 fatty acids, polyphenols, or whole-food fibre; whole fruit is preferred for its fibre and antioxidant content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Juice concentrate (any flavour) is a processed beverage ingredient produced by removing the majority of water from fresh fruit juice through evaporation or freeze-concentration, resulting in a thick, syrupy liquid with intensified flavour and sugar content. It is available in a wide range of fruit flavours including orange, apple, grape, tropical, and mixed berry. Before consumption, the concentrate is typically diluted with water at a ratio of approximately 1:4 to 1:6 to yield a ready-to-drink juice beverage. Per 100 g of concentrate, the nutritional profile shows 38 kcal energy, 9.0 g carbohydrate, 10.41 g sugar, 0 g protein, 0 g fat, 0 g dietary fibre, and 3 mg sodium. The sugar content exceeds 10 g per 100 g, classifying this product as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under Singapore HPB and WHO definitions. The absence of dietary fibre means that the concentrated fruit sugars — primarily fructose and glucose — are absorbed rapidly into the bloodstream without the moderating effect of fibre present in whole fruit. Clinically, juice concentrates are of concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis due to their high free-sugar load and lack of protective nutrients. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 66 (medium GI), but the concentrated sugar delivery makes glycaemic load significant at typical serving sizes. Juice concentrates are plant-based, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free, and are widely used in Singapore and globally as a convenient beverage base, cordial, or flavouring agent in food service and home settings.

Is juice concentrate better than whole fruit?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D03070015) at confidence 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. All core macros and secondary nutrients are directly reported. GI is an analogous estimate (medium confidence component) but the overall record confidence remains high given the exact HPB source match for nutritional data.
notes: protein_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database (D03070015); juice concentrate is a carbohydrate-dominant processed beverage with no protein by composition. fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database (D03070015); juice concentrate contains no fat by formulation — confirmed zero. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero consistent with zero total fat. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source; the concentration process removes fibre present in whole fruit. calcium_mg and iron_mg are reported as 0.0 per HPB source. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based product with no animal-derived components. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for this generic juice concentrate category are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry D03070015 as of 2026. Note on sugar vs carbohydrate anomaly: the HPB source reports sugar (10.41 g/100 g) slightly exceeding total carbohydrate (9.0 g/100 g). This is a known data artefact in some HPB entries where sugar is measured independently and rounding differences occur; values are preserved as reported from the approved source. GI of 66 is an analogous estimate based on published GI values for fruit juice drinks from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008); no direct GI measurement exists for this generic multi-flavour concentrate. This food is classified as a generic record (record_type: generic) covering any flavour of juice concentrate; flavour-specific nutritional variation may exist. No cuisine-specific multilingual names are required as this is a generic international beverage product with no specific cultural or regional origin.
